title: His Phoenix
content:
There is a queen in China, or maybe itï¿½s in Spain,
And birthdays and holidays such praises can be heard
Of her unblemished lineaments, a whiteness with no stain,
That she might be that sprightly girl who was trodden by a bird;
And thereï¿½s a score of duchesses, surpassing womankind,
Or who have found a painter to make them so for pay
And smooth out stain and blemish with the elegance of his mind:
I knew a phoenix in my youth so let them have their day.

The young men every night applaud their Gabyï¿½s laughing eye,
And Ruth St. Denis had more charm although she had poor luck;
From nineteen hundred nine or ten, Pavlovaï¿½s had the cry,
And thereï¿½s a player in the States who gathers up her cloak
And flings herself out of the room when Juliet would be bride
With all a womanï¿½s passion, a childï¿½s imperious way,
And there areï¿½but no matter if there are scores beside:
I knew a phoenix in my youth so let them have their day.

Thereï¿½s Margaret and Marjorie and Dorothy and Nan,
A Daphne and a Mary who live in privacy;
Oneï¿½s had her fill of lovers, anotherï¿½s had but one,
Another boasts, ï¿½I pick and choose and have but two or three.ï¿½
If head and limb have beauty and the instepï¿½s high and light,
They can spread out what sail they please for all I have to say,
Be but the breakers of menï¿½s hearts or engines of delight:
I knew a phoenix in my youth so let them have their day.

Thereï¿½ll be that crowd to make men wild through all the centuries,
And maybe thereï¿½ll be some young belle walk out to make men wild
Who is my beautyï¿½s equal, though that my heart denies,
But not the exact likeness, the simplicity of a child,
And that proud look as though she had gazed into the burning sun,
And all the shapely body no tittle gone astray,
I mourn for that most lonely thing; and yet Godï¿½s will be done,
I knew a phoenix in my youth, so let them have their day.
meaning:
This evocative piece by William Butler Yeats represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of William Butler Yeats's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
